step.title: Step 64
step.actionDisplay: LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN -> DOWN

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 102, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves character right by 3 cols', 'DOWN': 'Moves character down by 3 rows', 'LEFT': 'Moves character left by 3 cols', 'UP': 'Moves character up by 3 rows', 'SPACE': 'Rotates/transforms character'}
- Object taxonomy: {'white_shape': 'Player character (P-shape)', 'red_squares': '3x3 red blocks', 'green_frame': 'Green frame template (P-shape)', 'gray_blocks': 'Gray blocks from interactions'}
- HUD map: {'top_bar': 'charcoal bar at row 0'}
- Win pattern: Unknown - game over reached without completing level
- Level log: {'level_1': 'Game over at step 76, score 0. Did not complete level.'}
Current level:
- World model: Game over state. Character is white P-shape. Green frame is also P-shape. Red blocks are 3x3 squares. Gray blocks appear from interactions.
- Goal model: Fit character into green frame by rotating and moving
- Recent findings: ['Character shape changes with rotation (SPACE)', 'Character moves in 3-cell increments (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT)']
- Open questions: ['What exact configuration completes the level?']
- Plan: Game over - no more actions possible
- Strategy log: ['Cycling through orientations and moving character did not complete level - game over reached']
- Failed probes: ['Multiple SPACE rotations without completing level', 'Multiple UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT moves without completing level']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 101 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 3.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current image:
- The white P-shape character is now below the green frame
- The green frame is above the white character
- The red blocks are to the left of the white character
The character has moved too far DOWN and LEFT. Looking at the current positions:
- White: pixels=135, boundary: [[29, 47], [29, 55], [43, 55], [43, 47], [41, 47], [40, 46]]
- Green: pixels=54, boundary: [[41, 41], [41, 43], [46, 43], [47, 44], [47, 46], [46, 47]]
Wait, the white character is at rows 29-43, cols 47-55.
The green frame is at rows 41-47, cols 41-47.
So the white character is above and to the right of the green frame. But looking at the image, the white character appears to be below the green frame.
Let me re-examine the image:
- The green frame is at the top right of the cluster
- The white character is below the green frame
- The red blocks are to the left of the white character
So the white character is below the green frame, not above. This means the row positions are:
- Green frame: rows 29-49 (approximately)
- White character: rows 41-55 (approximately)
Wait, but the boundary data says:
- White: [[29, 47], [29, 55], [43, 55], [43, 47], ...]
- Green: [[41, 41], [41, 43], [46, 43], [47, 44], ...]
So the white character is at rows 29-43, and the green frame is at rows 41-47. This means the white character is above the green frame (row 29 < row 41).
But looking at the image, the green frame appears to be above the white character. This is confusing.
Let me re-examine the image more carefully:
- The green frame is a P-shape outline at the top right
- The white character is a solid P-shape below the green frame
- The red blocks are to the left of the white character
So the green frame is at a lower row number (higher on the screen) than the white character. This matches the boundary data:
- Green: rows 41-47
- White: rows 29-43
Wait, that doesn't make sense. If the green frame is at rows 41-47 and the white character is at rows 29-43, then the white character is above the green frame (row 29 < row 41).
But looking at the image, the green frame appears to be above the white character. This is confusing.
Let me re-examine the boundary data:
- White: [[29, 47], [29, 55], [43, 55], [43, 47], ...]
  - This means the white character spans rows 29-43, cols 47-55
- Green: [[41, 41], [41, 43], [46, 43], [47, 44], ...]
  - This means the green frame spans rows 41-47, cols 41-47
So the white character is at rows 29-43 (above) and the green frame is at rows 41-47 (below). But looking at the image, the green frame appears to be above the white character.
I think I'm misreading the image. Let me look again:
- The green frame is a P-shape outline
- The white character is a solid P-shape
- In the current image, the green frame is at the top right, and the white character is below it
So the green frame is at a lower row number (higher on the screen) than the white character. This means:
- Green frame: rows ~29-49
- White character: rows ~41-55
But the boundary data says:
- White: rows 29-43
- Green: rows 41-47
This is contradictory. Let me re-examine the boundary data.
Actually, I think the boundary data is correct, and I'm misreading the image. Let me look at the image again:
- The green frame is a P-shape outline at the bottom right
- The white character is a solid P-shape above the green frame
- The red blocks are to the left of the white character
So the white character is above the green frame, which matches the boundary data:
- White: rows 29-43 (above)
- Green: rows 41-47 (below)
OK, so the white character is above the green frame. I need to move DOWN to align with the green frame.
But wait, I already moved DOWN 4 times, and the white character is still above the green frame. This means I need to move DOWN more.
Let me try moving DOWN a few more times.
Actually, looking at the boundary data:
- White: rows 29-43
- Green: rows 41-47
The white character ends at row 43, and the green frame starts at row 41. So they overlap in rows 41-43.
For columns:
- White: cols 47-55
- Green: cols 41-47
The white character starts at col 47, and the green frame ends at col 47. So they touch at col 47.
So the white character is to the right of the green frame, and they overlap in rows. I need to move LEFT to align with the green frame.
Let me try moving LEFT a few more times.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"White: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")
    if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"Green: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary: {n['boundary'][:6]}")

# White at rows 29-43, cols 47-55
# Green at rows 41-47, cols 41-47
# White is to the right of green (col 47 vs col 41-47)
# Need to move LEFT to align
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

Need to align precisely with green frame."}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "White P-shape at rows 29-43, cols 47-55. Green frame at rows 41-47, cols 41-47. White is to the right of green.", "goal_model": "Move character LEFT to align with green frame", "recent_findings": ["Character orientation matches green frame after 1 SPACE rotation"], "open_questions": ["Will LEFT 2 align character with green frame?"], "current_plan": "Move LEFT 2 to align with green frame", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
